name: Acne Vulgaris

key_questions: Severity: comedonal (blackheads/whiteheads only), mild inflammatory (few papules/pustules), moderate inflammatory (multiple papules/pustules), or severe (many papules/pustules + nodules/cysts)?
Current treatments tried (OTC retinoids, benzoyl peroxide, niacinamide)?
Female: relation to menstrual cycle? PCOS features?
Medications worsening acne: corticosteroids, lithium, iodides, androgens?
red_flags: Acne fulminans: sudden onset severe nodular acne + fever + arthralgia → urgent dermatology
Signs of PCOS in women: irregular menses, hirsutism → appropriate investigation
Psychological impact (anxiety, depression, social withdrawal) — address actively

first_line.name: Scope Note
first_line.notes: ⚠️ Acne vulgaris is NOT in the Ontario MA prescribing scope. Pharmacists can counsel on OTC products and refer. Topical clindamycin/BPO combination and oral therapies require physician/NP prescription.

first_line.name: Benzoyl Peroxide
first_line.notes: Most effective OTC acne treatment. Bactericidal (no resistance), comedolytic, anti-inflammatory. Start with 2.5% (less irritating than 5% or 10% — same efficacy). Apply to entire affected area (not just spots). Bleaches fabrics — warn patient. Takes 6–8 weeks for full benefit. Application sequencing: apply BPO in the MORNING; apply retinoid in the EVENING — avoid co-applying as BPO can oxidise retinoids.
first_line.preferred_when: Mild inflammatory acne (papules/pustules); also prevents antibiotic resistance when combined with topical antibiotics

first_line.name: Topical Retinoid
first_line.drugs: Adapalene 0.1% gel (Differin — OTC) OD at bedtime | Adapalene 0.3% gel (Differin 0.3% — Rx only) | Tretinoin 0.025–0.05% cream (Retin-A — prescription) | Trifarotene 0.005% cream (Aklief — prescription)
first_line.notes: Normalize follicular keratinization, reduce comedones, anti-inflammatory. Adapalene 0.1% (Differin): OTC in Canada — excellent tolerability. Adapalene 0.3%: prescription only — do not confuse with OTC 0.1% strength. Tretinoin: prescription, slightly more potent. Trifarotene 0.005% cream (Aklief): Health Canada approved 2020; indicated for body acne (face, trunk, shoulders) in patients ≥9 years — requires prescription. Apply pea-sized amount to ENTIRE face/affected area (not spot treatment) at bedtime. Initial irritation (dryness, peeling) peaks weeks 2–4 then improves. Sun protection essential. Takes 12+ weeks for full benefit. Pregnancy safety: AVOID all topical retinoids in pregnancy — teratogenic. Safe alternatives: azelaic acid, BPO, erythromycin.
first_line.preferred_when: Comedonal acne (blackheads/whiteheads); moderate acne; long-term maintenance

second_line.name: Topical Antibiotic + BPO Combination
second_line.drugs: Clindamycin 1% + benzoyl peroxide 5% gel (Benzaclin, Clindoxyl ADV) — apply OD (once daily)
second_line.notes: Clindamycin 1% topical: anti-inflammatory, reduces P. acnes. ALWAYS combine with benzoyl peroxide to prevent antibiotic resistance. Never use topical antibiotic alone (without BPO) — resistance risk. AAD 2024: fixed-dose combinations (e.g. Clindoxyl ADV) support OD dosing — BID dosing increases irritation without added benefit.

second_line.name: Oral Antibiotic (Rx — physician/NP required)
second_line.drugs: Doxycycline 50–100 mg BID OR 100 mg OD (modified-release, e.g. Apprilon) | Minocycline 50–100 mg BID (second choice)
second_line.notes: Use for ≤3–4 months maximum; always combine with topical BPO to prevent resistance. Doxycycline: preferred tetracycline (lower resistance profile than minocycline). Minocycline: second choice due to greater resistance concerns and drug-induced lupus risk. Take with food and full glass of water; avoid lying down for 30 min (esophageal irritation). AVOID in pregnancy (tetracyclines contraindicated in pregnancy and children <8 years).

second_line.name: Hormonal Therapy — Adult Women (Rx — physician/NP required)
second_line.drugs: Spironolactone 50–200 mg/day | Cyproterone acetate/ethinyl estradiol (Diane-35) | OCP: ethinyl estradiol + drospirenone (Yaz, Yasmin) | EE + norgestimate (Tri-Cyclen)
second_line.notes: For adult women with hormonal/cyclical acne ± PCOS. Spironolactone: anti-androgen; effective for hormonal acne; requires monitoring of potassium/BP; contraindicated in pregnancy (teratogenic — reliable contraception required). Cyproterone/EE (Diane-35): Health Canada approved for acne in women — note increased VTE risk vs other OCPs; contraindicated in women with migraine with aura. Combined OCPs: reduces androgenic stimulation of sebaceous glands; takes 3+ months for full benefit.

second_line.name: Azelaic Acid
second_line.drugs: Azelaic acid 15% gel (Finacea) or 20% cream (Skinoren) BID
second_line.notes: Comedolytic, antibacterial, anti-inflammatory; also reduces post-inflammatory hyperpigmentation (PIH). Pregnancy-safe (suitable alternative when retinoids and tetracyclines must be avoided). Requires prescription in Canada.

second_line.name: Combined OCP (females)
second_line.drugs: Ethinyl estradiol + drospirenone (Yaz, Yasmin) | EE + norgestimate (Tri-Cyclen)
second_line.notes: For women with acne ± PCOS — reduces androgenic stimulation of sebaceous glands. Health Canada approved for acne. Takes 3+ months for full benefit.

non_pharm: Gentle non-comedogenic cleanser BID (not scrubbing — worsens inflammation)
Non-comedogenic SPF 30+ sunscreen (especially with retinoid use)
Do not pick or squeeze (causes scarring, spreads bacteria)
Application sequencing: MORNING — apply benzoyl peroxide; EVENING — apply retinoid. Avoid co-application (BPO oxidises retinoids, reducing efficacy and increasing irritation)
Pregnancy safety note: avoid all retinoids (topical and oral) and tetracyclines. Acceptable options: azelaic acid, benzoyl peroxide, topical erythromycin
Manage psychological impact — refer if significant mental health effect
refer_when: Moderate-to-severe inflammatory acne requiring prescription therapy (topical antibiotics, oral antibiotics, retinoids)
Nodulocystic acne — candidate for oral isotretinoin (Accutane/Epuris) — requires dermatologist
Women requesting hormonal therapy (spironolactone, Diane-35, OCP for acne)
Acne in pregnancy — safe prescribing guidance needed
Scarring — escalate treatment to prevent further damage
Acne with significant psychological distress (anxiety, depression, social withdrawal)

references: Ontario College of Pharmacists. Minor Ailments: Acne. OCP Prescribing Standards, O. Reg. 256/24 (2025).
Canadian Dermatology Association (CDA). Acne Management Guidelines. J Cutan Med Surg. 2015;19(2):101–108 (latest CDA guidance, with OCP updates).
Zaenglein AL, et al. Guidelines of care for the management of acne vulgaris. J Am Acad Dermatol. 2016;74(5):945–973.e33. (AAD, updated 2024)
NICE. Acne vulgaris: management. NICE guideline [NG198]. National Institute for Health and Care Excellence (2021).
